examples.title: FIFO Page Replacement Algorithm
examples.description: Implementation of First-In-First-Out page replacement algorithm
examples.code:
def fifo_page_replacement(pages, frames):
    page_faults = 0
    memory = []  # represents frames in memory
    
    for page in pages:
        if page not in memory:
            page_faults += 1
            if len(memory) < frames:
                memory.append(page)
            else:
                # Remove oldest page (first inserted)
                memory.pop(0)
                memory.append(page)
        print(f"Page {page}: Frames {memory}, Fault: {page_faults > 0}")
    
    return page_faults

# Example usage
pages = [7, 0, 1, 2, 0, 3, 0, 4, 2, 3, 0, 3, 2]
frames = 3
print(f"Total Page Faults: {fifo_page_replacement(pages, frames)}")
examples.explanation: This Python implementation simulates FIFO page replacement. When a page fault occurs and all frames are occupied, the page that entered memory first is replaced. It maintains a list representing frames and uses pop(0) to remove the oldest page.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n × f) where n = number of pages, f = number of frames
examples.spaceComplexity: O(f) where f = number of frames

examples.title: LRU Page Replacement Algorithm
examples.description: C implementation of Least Recently Used page replacement
examples.explanation: This C implementation uses a time counter to track page usage. When a page fault occurs, it finds the page with the smallest time value (Least Recently Used) and replaces it. Each page access updates the time counter.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n × f) where n = page references, f = number of frames
examples.spaceComplexity: O(f) for frame array and time tracking

examples.title: Optimal Page Replacement Algorithm
examples.description: Implementation of Optimal (OPT) page replacement - theoretical best
examples.explanation: The optimal algorithm replaces the page that will not be used for the longest time in the future. This gives the minimum possible page faults but requires knowledge of future page references, making it impractical for real systems. It serves as a theoretical benchmark.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n² × f) - checks all future references for each replacement

examples.title: Memory Allocation - First Fit
examples.description: Java implementation of First Fit contiguous memory allocation
examples.explanation: First Fit allocates memory to the first available block that is large enough. It scans memory from the beginning and stops at the first fit. This algorithm is fast but may lead to external fragmentation as it leaves small blocks scattered throughout memory.
examples.timeComplexity: O(m × p) where m = memory blocks, p = processes
examples.spaceComplexity: O(m + p) for memory blocks and process tracking
